---
title: "Forecasting Dengue:  Journey Through Challenges, Actions, and Lessons Learned"
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: [default]
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    slide-number: true
    title-slide-attributes: 
      data-background-color: "#3f007d"
      data-background-image: none
    css: styles.css
author:
  - name: Thiyanga S. Talagala
    affiliations: Department of Statistics, University of Sri Jayewardenepura, Sri Lanka 
subtitle: "Women in Forecasting (WIF) Conference - 2026, Montreal, Canada"
---

```{r}
# Helper packages - need dplyr and ggplot
library(denguedatahub)
# library(kableExtra)
data("srilanka_weekly_data")
library(tsibble)
library(feasts)
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(kableExtra)
library(knitr)
library(lubridate)
library(patchwork)
# install.packages("devtools")
#devtools::install_github("thiyangt/SriLanka")
library(sf)
library(SriLanka)
```



## {background-image="map1.png" background-size="cover"}

## {background-image="map2.png" background-size="cover"}

## {background-image="map3.png" background-size="cover"}

## Sri Lanka Climate 

🌴Climate type

- Tropical climate: Warm and humid throughout the year

 ☀️Average temperature
 
  - Typically 26°C – 30°C in lowlands
    
  - Cooler in central highlands (e.g., Nuwara Eliya ~16°C–20°C)

 🌧💧️ Rainfall


  - Two main monsoon seasons: 
  
    - Southwest monsoon (May–September)
    
    - Northeast monsoon (December–February)
    
    
 
# {background-image="map4.jpg" background-size="contain"} 





# {background-image="map5.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}


#  {background-image="map6.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# What makes dengue forecasting a challenging problem?



## Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka: 2007 - 2016


```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(denguedatahub)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date),
    year = year(start.date)
  ) %>%
  group_by(start.date, year) %>%
  summarise(island_cases = sum(cases, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(period = ifelse(year <= 2016, "≤ 2017", "> 2017")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = start.date, y = island_cases, color = period)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("≤ 2017" = "black", "> 2017" = "white")) +
  #theme_bw() +
  labs(
   # title = "Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka (Island Total)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Cases",
    color = "Period"
  ) + theme(legend.position = "none")
```


## Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka: 2007 - 2018


```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(denguedatahub)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date),
    year = year(start.date)
  ) %>%
  group_by(start.date, year) %>%
  summarise(island_cases = sum(cases, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(period = ifelse(year <= 2018, "≤ 2017", "> 2017")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = start.date, y = island_cases, color = period)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("≤ 2017" = "black", "> 2017" = "white")) +
  #theme_bw() +
  labs(
   # title = "Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka (Island Total)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Cases",
    color = "Period"
  ) + theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka: 2007 - 2019


```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(denguedatahub)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date),
    year = year(start.date)
  ) %>%
  group_by(start.date, year) %>%
  summarise(island_cases = sum(cases, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(period = ifelse(year <= 2020, "≤ 2017", "> 2017")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = start.date, y = island_cases, color = period)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("≤ 2017" = "black", "> 2017" = "white")) +
  #theme_bw() +
  labs(
   # title = "Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka (Island Total)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Cases",
    color = "Period"
  ) + theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka: 2007 - 2022


```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(denguedatahub)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date),
    year = year(start.date)
  ) %>%
  group_by(start.date, year) %>%
  summarise(island_cases = sum(cases, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(period = ifelse(year <= 2022, "≤ 2017", "> 2017")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = start.date, y = island_cases, color = period)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("≤ 2017" = "black", "> 2017" = "white")) +
  #theme_bw() +
  labs(
   # title = "Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka (Island Total)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Cases",
    color = "Period"
  ) + theme(legend.position = "none")
```



## Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka: 2007 - 2026 March


```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(denguedatahub)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(
    start.date = mdy(start.date),
    year = year(start.date)
  ) %>%
  group_by(start.date, year) %>%
  summarise(island_cases = sum(cases, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(period = ifelse(year <= 2027, "≤ 2017", "> 2017")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = start.date, y = island_cases, color = period)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("≤ 2017" = "black", "> 2017" = "white")) +
  #theme_bw() +
  labs(
   # title = "Weekly Dengue Cases in Sri Lanka (Island Total)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Cases",
    color = "Period"
  ) + theme(legend.position = "none")
```

# Forecasting models rely on the assumption that historical data pattern continues into the future.  

# 

### Weekly Dengue Cases by District (2020 onwards)

```{r}
#| fig.width: 15
#| fig.height: 8
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(viridis)
library(scales)

srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(start.date = mdy(start.date)) %>%
  filter(start.date >= as.Date("2020-01-01")) %>%
  group_by(district, start.date) %>%
  summarise(cases = sum(cases, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = start.date, y = district, fill = cases)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "C",
    limits = c(1, NA),
    breaks = c(1, 10, 50, 100, 200),   # marks on scale
    oob = scales::squish
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Time",
    y = "District",
    fill = "Cases"
  )
```


# Dengue is endemic in all districts across the country throughout the year.

# {background-image="sp.png" background-size="contain"} 


#

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(start.date = mdy(start.date)) %>%
  filter(district %in% c("Colombo", "Gampaha", "Kalutara")) %>%
  arrange(start.date) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = start.date, y = cases)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.6) +
  facet_grid(rows = vars(district), scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Weekly Dengue Cases: Colombo, Gampaha, Kalutara",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Number of Cases"
  ) +
 # theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

#

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| fig.width: 5
#| fig.height: 7
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(SriLanka)

data("lka_adm2")

west_districts <- lka_adm2 %>%
  filter(NAME %in% c("Colombo District",
                     "Gampaha District",
                     "Kalutara District"))

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = lka_adm2, color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = west_districts, fill = "steelblue", color = "black", linewidth = 0.4)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Even though Colombo, Gampaha, and Kalutara are geographically close in the Western Province,
their dengue time series patterns can look quite different.

- Spatial proximity does not imply temporal similarity in dengue dynamics.
:::
::::


# {background-image="map7.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# There is no centralized repository for accessing tidy dengue data.

# Action 1: Dengue Data Hub

# {background-image="map8.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# {background-image="we.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

## `denguedatahub` R package


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

```{r}
#| echo: true
#pak::pak("thiyangt/denguedatahub")
#install.packages("denguedatahub")
library(denguedatahub)
srilanka_weekly_data
```

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

![](logo.png)

:::
::::

#

### https://thiyangt.github.io/denguedatahubweb/

```{r}
#| fig.height: 4
knitr::include_graphics("cover.png")
```

We have data for 225 countries around the world.

# {background-image="web1.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# {background-image="web.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# Action 2: Dengue Atlas

[https://github.com/thiyangt/DengueAtlas](https://github.com/thiyangt/DengueAtlas)

# {background-image="a1.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# {background-image="a2.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# {background-image="a3.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# {background-image="a4.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}

# {background-image="a5.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}


# There is no single model–training dataset combination that works well all the time.




## Action 3:  Impact of training dataset and model choice on dengue forecasting

i. How do different training dataset choices and different model choices influence forecast error?



## Action 3:  Impact of training dataset and model choice on dengue forecasting

i. How do different training dataset choices and different model choices influence forecast error?

ii. Do training dataset and model choices exhibit spatial patterns across districts?





## Action 3:  Impact of training dataset and model choice on dengue forecasting

i. How do different training dataset choices and different model choices influence forecast error?

ii. Do training dataset and model choices exhibit spatial patterns across districts?

iii. Are the strengths of seasonality and the strengths of trend, computed over different time periods, helpful in identifying the best-performing combination of training dataset and model?



## Action 3:  Impact of training dataset and model choice on dengue forecasting

i. How do different training dataset choices and different model choices influence forecast error?

ii. Do training dataset and model choices exhibit spatial patterns across districts?

iii. Are the strengths of seasonality and the strengths of trend, computed over different time periods, helpful in identifying the best-performing combination of training dataset and model?

iv. Do the strength of seasonality and the strength of trend, computed over different time periods, help explain the choice of the best model and training dataset?

#

```{r}

colombo <- srilanka_weekly_data |>
  group_by(year, week, start.date) |>
  filter(year > 2017) |>
  filter(district == "Colombo") |>
  mutate(
    yearweek = yearweek(start.date)) |>
  distinct(yearweek, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  mutate(highlight = yearweek >= yearweek("2019 W47") & yearweek <= yearweek("2022 W52")) 

country_weekly_tsibble <- colombo |>
  as_tsibble(index = yearweek) |>
  mutate(highlight = yearweek >= yearweek("2019 W47") & yearweek <= yearweek("2022 W52"))

  ggplot(data = colombo) +
  geom_line(
            aes(x = yearweek, y = cases),
            color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(data = country_weekly_tsibble %>% filter(highlight),
            aes(x = yearweek, y = cases),
            color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_x_yearweek(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Weekly Dengue Cases"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

```



#


### Training set

i. Pre COVID-19 period: 2007 - 2019

ii. During COVID-19 period: 2020 - 2022 

iii. Post COVID-19 period: 2023 - 2024 

### Test set

The first 8 weeks of 2025

#

## Training Dataset Choices

- All data

- Use only post COVID-19 period data

- Forecasting the interrupted period first and then using it for modeling

- Down-weight the interrupted period

## Model Choices

i. **Automated ARIMA algorithms**

ii. **Trigonometric seasonality, Box-Cox transformation, ARMA errors, Trend and Seasonal components (TBATS) model** 

iii. **STL - ETS approach:**  Using STL decomposition, the seasonal component is extracted from the original series. Then, the ETS model is fitted to the seasonally adjusted series. When fitting the ETS model, the seasonal component is set to "None". In this way, STL captures seasonality, ETS models the remaining structure, and the final forecast is their combination.   


iii. **Dynamic Harmonic Regression (DHR):** Complex seasonality is modeled using Fourier terms combined with ARIMA errors.

#

```{r data, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
#| fig.height: 10
#ARIMA
ARIMA_Benchmark_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ARIMA_Benchmark_accuracy.rds"))
ARIMA_downweight_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ARIMA_downweight_accuracy.rds"))
ARIMA_excludecovid_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ARIMA_excludecovid_accuracy.rds"))
ARIMA_updated_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ARIMA_updated_accuracy.rds"))
#ETS
ETS_Benchmark_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ETS_Benchmark_accuracy.rds"))
ETS_downweight_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ETS_downweight_accuracy.rds"))
ETS_excludecovid_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ETS_excludecovid_accuracy.rds"))
ETS_updated_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "ETS_updated_accuracy.rds"))
#dhr
dhr_Benchmark_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "dhr_Benchmark_accuracy.rds"))
dhr_downweight_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "dhr_downweight_accuracy.rds"))
dhr_excludecovid_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "dhr_excludecovid_accuracy.rds"))
dhr_updated_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "dhr_updated_accuracy.rds"))
#TABTS
TBATS_Benchmark_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "TBATS_Benchmark_accuracy.rds"))
TBATS_Benchmark_accuracy$.model <- c("tbats")
TBATS_downweight_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "TBATS_downweight_accuracy.rds"))
TBATS_excludecovid_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "TBATS_excludecovid_accuracy.rds"))
TBATS_updated_accuracy <- readRDS(here("results", "TBATS_updated_accuracy.rds"))
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
# processing data
District <- rep(ARIMA_Benchmark_accuracy$district, times = 16)
Data <- rep(c("Full", "Downweight", "Excluded", "Updated"), each=26, times =4)
Model <- rep(c("ARIMA", "ETS", "DHR", "TBATS"), each=104)
RMSE <- c(ARIMA_Benchmark_accuracy$RMSE,
ARIMA_downweight_accuracy$RMSE,
ARIMA_excludecovid_accuracy$RMSE,
ARIMA_updated_accuracy$RMSE,
ETS_Benchmark_accuracy$RMSE,
ETS_downweight_accuracy$RMSE,
ETS_excludecovid_accuracy$RMSE,
ETS_updated_accuracy$RMSE,
dhr_Benchmark_accuracy$RMSE,
dhr_downweight_accuracy$RMSE,
dhr_excludecovid_accuracy$RMSE,
dhr_updated_accuracy$RMSE,
TBATS_Benchmark_accuracy$RMSE,
TBATS_downweight_accuracy$RMSE,
TBATS_excludecovid_accuracy$RMSE,
TBATS_updated_accuracy$RMSE)
data <- tibble(District, Data, Model, RMSE)
data_clean <- data |>
  mutate(RMSE = ifelse(is.nan(RMSE), NA, RMSE))
#table(data_clean$Model, data$Data)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
df_best_flag <- data_clean %>%
  group_by(District) %>%
  mutate(
    Best = if_else(RMSE == min(RMSE, na.rm = TRUE), "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  ungroup()


```

```{r}
#| fig.width: 15
#| fig.height: 6
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Step 0: Replace NaN with NA
data_clean <- data %>%
 mutate(RMSE = ifelse(is.nan(RMSE), NA, RMSE))

# Step 1: Select best model per District–Data
df_best_model <- data_clean %>%
  group_by(District, Data) %>%
  filter(RMSE == min(RMSE, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  slice(1) %>%                # handle ties
  ungroup()

# Step 2: Add alpha column
# Minimum RMSE across Data within each District → alpha = 1
# Others → alpha = 0.5
df_best_model <- df_best_model %>%
  group_by(District) %>%
  arrange(RMSE, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    alpha_val = ifelse(row_number() == 1, 1, 0.4)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Step 3: Ensure all model levels are retained for color mapping
df_best_model <- df_best_model %>%
  mutate(Model = factor(Model, 
                        levels = c("ARIMA", "ETS", "DHR", "TBATS"),
                        labels = c("ARIMA", "STL-ETS", "DHR", "TBATS")))

# Step 4: Plot
p1 <- ggplot(df_best_model, aes(x = Data, y = District)) +
  geom_tile(aes(fill = Model, alpha = alpha_val),
            color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(is.na(RMSE), "-", 
                               sprintf("%.2f", RMSE))),
            color = "black",
            size = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  )) +
  scale_alpha_identity() +   # uses alpha values directly
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
  #  title = "District-wise RMSE by Data",
  #  subtitle = "Fully opaque tile = lowest RMSE within district",
    title = "(a)",
 #   fill = "Model",
    x = "Training dataset"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

library(dplyr)

df_min_rmse <- df_best_model %>%
  group_by(District) %>%
  slice_min(order_by = RMSE, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() 
  

#df_min_rmse

lka_adm2 <- lka_adm2 |>
  mutate(join_key = substr(NAME, 1, 5))
df_min_rmse  <- df_min_rmse  |>
  mutate(join_key = substr(District, 1, 5)) 
# left join to preserve sf geometry from lka_adm2
combined_sf <- lka_adm2 %>%
  left_join(df_min_rmse , by = "join_key")
p2 <- ggplot(combined_sf ) + 
  geom_sf(aes(fill=Model)) + scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  )) + labs(title = "(b)")
p3 <- ggplot(combined_sf ) + 
  geom_sf(aes(fill=Data)) + scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Downweight" = "#7fc97f", 
    "Full"   = "#beaed4", 
    "Updated"   = "#fdc086", 
    "Excluded" = "#1f78b4"
  )) + labs(title = "(c)", fill = "Training dataset")
p1|p2|p3
```

Figure 1: (a) Best forecasting model by district under different training sets, (b) Best forecasting model for each district, (c) Best training set for each district.

#

```{r}
#| fig.height: 5
p1
```

Figure 1: (a) Best forecasting model by district under different training sets

#

```{r}
#| fig.width: 15
#| fig.height: 6
p1|p2|p3
```

Figure 1: (a) Best forecasting model by district under different training sets, (b) Best forecasting model for each district, (c) Best training set for each district.


# {background-image="two.png" background-size="contain"} 







# Time Series Features

- **Cognostics**: **Co**mputer-aided dia**gnostics** (John W. Tukey, 1985)

- Characteristics of time series

- Summary measures of time series


**Basic Principle**

- Transform a given time series $y=\{y_1, y_2, \cdots, y_n\}$ into a feature vector $F = (f_1(y), f_2(y), \cdots, f_p(y))'$. 


#

## Time Series Features

**strength of the trend** 

$$ F_T = \max \left(0,\; 1 - \frac{\operatorname{Var}(R_t)}{\operatorname{Var}(T_t + R_t)} \right)$$



**strength of seasonality** 


$$F_S = \max \left(0,\; 1 - \frac{\operatorname{Var}(R_t)}{\operatorname{Var}(S_t + R_t)} \right)$$

$\operatorname{Var}(R_t)$ - variance of the remainder component

$\operatorname{Var}(T_t + R_t)$ - variance after removing the seasonal component

$\operatorname{Var}(S_t + R_t)$ - variance of the detrended series


# 

![](f1.png)

#

![](f2.png)

#

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(tsibble)

data_segmented <- srilanka_weekly_data %>%
  mutate(
    yearweek = yearweek(start.date)) |>
  mutate(
    period = case_when(
      year(yearweek) < 2020 ~ "Pre-COVID",
      year(yearweek) >= 2020 & year(yearweek) < 2023 ~ "COVID",
      year(yearweek) > 2022 ~ "Post-COVID"
    )
  )

library(feasts)
library(fabletools)

data_tsibble <- data_segmented %>%
  as_tsibble(key = c(district, period), index = yearweek) 

stl_features <- data_tsibble %>%
  features(cases, feat_stl)
#stl_features

df_combined_fea_best <- stl_features %>%
  left_join(df_min_rmse, by = c("district" = "District"))
```

```{r featureplot, fig.height=9, fig.width=7, fig.cap="\\label{featureplot} Figure 2: Feature-based exploration: The first column illustrates the strength of the trend across different periods, the second column depicts the strength of seasonality across periods, and the third column shows pairwise plots of the first three principal components."}
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

df_wide <- df_combined_fea_best %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = period,
    values_from = c(trend_strength, seasonal_strength_year)
  )

library(dplyr)

# Trend variables
trend_covid <- df_wide %>%
  select(district, trend_strength_COVID, Data, Model) %>%
  drop_na()

trend_post <- df_wide %>%
  select(district, `trend_strength_Post-COVID`, Data, Model) %>%
  drop_na()

trend_pre <- df_wide %>%
  select(district, `trend_strength_Pre-COVID`, Data, Model) %>%
  drop_na()

# Seasonal variables
season_covid <- df_wide %>%
  select(district, seasonal_strength_year_COVID, Data, Model) %>%
  drop_na()

season_post <- df_wide %>%
  select(district, `seasonal_strength_year_Post-COVID`, Data, Model) %>%
  drop_na()

season_pre <- df_wide %>%
  select(district, `seasonal_strength_year_Pre-COVID`, Data, Model) %>%
  drop_na()

df_combined <- trend_covid %>%
  inner_join(trend_post, by = "district") %>%
  inner_join(trend_pre, by = "district") %>%
  inner_join(season_covid, by = "district") %>%
  inner_join(season_post, by = "district") %>%
  inner_join(season_pre, by = "district")


library(ggplot2)

# --- Trend plots ---
p1 <- ggplot(df_combined, aes(x = `trend_strength_Pre-COVID`, 
                          y = trend_strength_COVID, colour = Model.x, shape = Data.x)) +
  geom_point(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  size=4
) + geom_point() +
   geom_text(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  aes(label = district),
  vjust = -1
)  +
  labs(title = "Trend",
       subtitle = "(a)",
       x = "Pre COVID-19",
       y= "During COVID-19") + 
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype="dashed") +
#  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
#  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 

p2 <- ggplot(df_combined, aes(x = `trend_strength_Pre-COVID`, 
                          y = `trend_strength_Post-COVID`, colour = Model.x, shape = Data.x)) +
   geom_point(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  size=4
) + geom_point() +
   geom_text(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  aes(label = district),
  vjust = -1
) +
geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title = "",
       subtitle = "(b)",
       x = "Pre COVID-19",
       y= "Post COVID-19") + 
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
 # geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1) +
 # scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
#  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 


p3 <- ggplot(df_combined, aes(x = trend_strength_COVID, 
                          y = `trend_strength_Post-COVID`, colour = Model.x, shape = Data.x)) +
   geom_point(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  size=4
) + geom_point() +
   geom_text(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  aes(label = district),
  vjust = -1
)+
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title = "",
       subtitle = "(c)",
       x = "During COVID-19",
       y= "Post COVID-19") + 
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
#  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1) +
#  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
#  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 


# --- Seasonal plots ---
p4 <- ggplot(df_combined, aes(x = `seasonal_strength_year_Pre-COVID`, 
                          y = seasonal_strength_year_COVID, colour = Model.x, shape = Data.x)) +
   geom_point(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  size=4
) + geom_point() +
   geom_text(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  aes(label = district),
  vjust = -1
)+
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title = "Seasonality",
       subtitle = "(d)",
       x = "Pre COVID-19",
       y= "During COVID-19") + 
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
#  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1) +
#  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
#  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 


p5 <- ggplot(df_combined, aes(x = `seasonal_strength_year_Pre-COVID`, 
                          y = `seasonal_strength_year_Post-COVID`, colour = Model.x, shape = Data.x)) +
   geom_point(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  size=4
) +
  geom_point() +
   geom_text(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  aes(label = district),
  vjust = -1
) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title = "",
       subtitle = "(e)",
       x = "Pre COVID-19",
       y= "Post COVID-19") + 
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
#  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1) +
#  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
#  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 


p6 <- ggplot(df_combined, aes(x = seasonal_strength_year_COVID, 
                          y = `seasonal_strength_year_Post-COVID`, colour = Model.x, shape = Data.x)) +
   geom_point(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  size=4
) + geom_point() +
   geom_text(
  data = df_combined[df_combined$district == "Colombo", ],
  aes(label = district),
  vjust = -1
) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title = "",
       subtitle = "(f)",
       x = "During COVID-19",
       y= "Post COVID-19",
       colour = "Forecast model",
       shape = "Training dataset") + 
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
#  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1) +
  #scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
 # scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
   guides(
    colour = guide_legend(order = 1, nrow = 1),
    shape  = guide_legend(order = 2, nrow = 1)
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical"  
  )


pcdf <- data.frame(tcovid = df_combined$trend_strength_COVID,
tpostcovid = df_combined$`trend_strength_Post-COVID`,
tprecovid = df_combined$`trend_strength_Pre-COVID`,
scovid = df_combined$seasonal_strength_year_COVID,
spostcovid = df_combined$`seasonal_strength_year_Post-COVID`,
sprecovid = df_combined$`seasonal_strength_year_Pre-COVID`)

pca_res <- prcomp(pcdf, scale. = TRUE)
scores <- as.data.frame(pca_res$x)
scores$Model <- df_combined$Model.x
scores$Data <- df_combined$Data.x

library(patchwork)
p1p2 <- ggplot(scores, aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, color = Model, shape=Data)) + geom_point() + 
 labs(title = "Principle Components",                                                                                          subtitle = "(g)",
       x = "PC1",
       y= "PC2") +
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
  theme(legend.position = "none")


p1p3 <- ggplot(scores, aes(x=PC1, y=PC3, color = Model, shape=Data)) + geom_point()  + labs(x="PC1", y="PC3", subtitle = "(h)",) +
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
  theme(legend.position = "none")


p2p3 <- ggplot(scores, aes(x=PC2, y=PC3, color = Model, shape=Data)) + geom_point() + labs(x="PC2", y="PC3", subtitle = "(i)") +
   scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "ARIMA" = "#1b9e77", 
    "STL-ETS"   = "#d95f02", 
    "DHR"   = "#7570b3", 
    "TBATS" = "#e7298a"
  ))+
  theme(legend.position = "none")


(p1 | p4 | p1p2) /
(p2 | p5 | p1p3) /
(p3 | p6| p2p3)
```



#

```{r}
p1|p4
```

## Lessons Learned

 - There is no single combination of training dataset and forecasting model that consistently delivers the best performance for a given time series across multiple time steps.

 - This suggests that early warning systems should be adaptive rather than static, and **should incorporate mechanisms to detect and respond to structural changes in disease transmission dynamics**.
 
#

###  Integrated Forecasting = Data  + Doctors  + Citizens 

```{r}
#| fig.height: 5
#| fig-align: center
knitr::include_graphics("vd.png")
```


# A citizen reporting portal for submitting dengue case information

# Kay Data Fields

1. Location

2. Patient demographics

3. Symptoms

4. Severity Assessment: Probably dengue, confirmed

5. Household size, recent travel history




## Action 4: Two-step forecasting model

**Step 1: What does the data suggest will happen?**

$$\hat{Y_t} = f(\text{climate},\text{time lags},\text{urbanization}, \text{mobility}, \text{interventions})$$

**Step 2: Does reality suggest something the model cannot see yet?**

$$
\hat{Y_t}^* =
g\left(
\text{prediction from step 1},
\substack{\text{clinical and epidemiological} \\
          \text{domain knowledge}}
\right)
$$

# {background-image="text.png" background-size="contain" background-repeat="no-repeat" background-position="center"}



#

## Lessons Learned

-  Local epidemiological knowledge can identify risks before they appear in the surveillance data.

- Human expertise and statistical models should complement, not replace, each other.

- Effective forecasting requires continuous monitoring, evaluation, and adaptation.

- Collaboration among epidemiologists, entomologists, clinicians, public health officials, and data scientists is essential.

## Acknowledgement

The Dengue Data Hub project was supported by funding from the R Consortium Infrastructure Steering Committee (ISC) Grant Program.

**Dengue Data Hub:** [https://thiyangt.github.io/denguedatahubweb/](https://thiyangt.github.io/denguedatahubweb/)

**Slides:** [https://thiyangt.github.io/wf2026/](https://thiyangt.github.io/wf2026/)

**Email:** ttalagala@sjp.ac.lk

```{r}
#| fig.height: 4
knitr::include_graphics("cover.png")
```

# {background-image="interaction.png" background-size="contain"}
